concepts_addressed: Daily Sales Role-Play as Training Cadence
Leadership Sets Culture; Culture Determines Acceptable Effort

frameworks.name: Sales Ops Checklist
frameworks.definition: A complete list of sales operations requirements — daily training, script memorization, script enforcement, and manager accountability — that together produce consistent close rates; missing any items and you do not get the outcome.

frameworks.name: Group Screening Funnel for Sales Reps
frameworks.definition: A three-stage hiring filter for sales reps: (1) send script before interview and request 60-second video recitation; (2) invite passing candidates to a group coaching call and evaluate coachability under live feedback; (3) extend job offers only to those who demonstrated preparation and coachability.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in a high-volume inbound sales business (17-rep team, Google Ads-driven leads, commission + base structure), revenue volatility — swinging from $700K peak months down to $200-250K the following month with consistent lead quality — is caused by a sales culture and discipline problem, not a lead quality problem.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Lead quality is constant month over month
Sales team on base + commission structure
Inbound lead source (Google Ads)
claims.mechanism: When lead quality is constant and revenue varies dramatically, the variance must originate from inconsistent sales execution — specifically varying script adherence and differing rep behaviors — rather than from external demand factors.

numbers.axis: monthly revenue swings for a real estate wholesaling business with consistent leads

bounded_by: Does not apply when lead quality changes between months — a change in lead source or quality requires different diagnosis

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the root cause of sales team inconsistency is always the sales manager or team leader, not individual reps, because sales culture is set from the top down and the leader determines which behaviors are reinforced and which carry consequences.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.topic: Leadership Sets Culture; Culture Determines Acceptable Effort
conditions: A dedicated sales manager exists on the team
Revenue volatility persists despite consistent leads
claims.mechanism: Culture is the aggregate of reinforced behaviors; the leader controls what behaviors are rewarded or punished. If reps go off-script without consequences, it is because the leader tolerates or implicitly rewards off-script behavior.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that script non-adherence is the observable signal of a sales culture problem, and that script adherence must be a non-negotiable employment condition — reps who deviate from the script cannot be permitted to take calls.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: if you are not saying the script word for word, you cannot be on this team
conditions: High-volume inbound sales operation
Script exists and has been provided to reps
claims.mechanism: When scripts are treated as suggestions rather than requirements, reps improvise, creating outcome variance; variance in process produces variance in close rates and makes the team non-replicable.
bounded_by: Speaker acknowledges this requires a leader capable of enforcing the standard — if the leader cannot enforce it, the constraint is the leader, not the reps

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that approximately 95-98% of sales teams lack understanding of sales training and sales culture, defaulting instead to a mercenary model — hire whoever closes deals, fire whoever does not — which produces high variance and eliminates team cohesion.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: The mercenary model creates a collection of individually-motivated lone operators with no shared process; one rep experiments with a tactic, closes a deal, and others copy it ad hoc — creating compounding process divergence and unpredictable outcomes.

numbers.axis: share of sales teams with genuine sales training and culture infrastructure

anchors.region: teams with real sales training culture
teams operating on mercenary model with no culture

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a sales team has a mercenary culture with no process enforcement, reps will independently improvise closing tactics, promise things fulfillment cannot deliver, and create long-term reputational and operational liability for the business.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: they start promising things and making deals that you can't cash
conditions: No script enforcement
No unified process
Commission-driven reps
claims.mechanism: Without a shared process, reps self-optimize for their own short-term commissions, which means they will say whatever closes the deal — including promises that create downstream customer expectations the business cannot fulfill.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the process must always rank above the individual player in a high-performance sales organization: no single rep is above the process, and any tolerance for exceptions destroys the cultural foundation that makes consistent performance possible.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: the process has to always go above the player
conditions: Sales team of meaningful size (10+ reps)
claims.mechanism: Allowing individual star reps to deviate from process signals to the team that performance results can override process compliance — this incentivizes all reps to experiment independently and erodes the common framework.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the training IP and ability to convert an unskilled person with only work ethic into a consistent closer — in approximately 14 days — is the core trade secret and primary competitive asset of a high-volume inbound sales business.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: that is your IP, that is the trade secret
conditions: High-volume inbound sales (e.g., real estate wholesaling)
Scripted, process-driven sales motion
claims.mechanism: If the training system can take a raw candidate with no sales skill and produce consistent closers in 14 days, the business is no longer constrained by talent availability; the bottleneck becomes the training system itself, which is reproducible and protectable.

numbers.axis: onboarding timeline to consistent closer from zero sales experience

anchors.region: closing deals consistently on inbound leads

bounded_by: Applies to scripted, process-driven inbound sales; does not apply to complex consultative sales requiring deep domain expertise

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that sales talent for a 17-person high-volume inbound team is a commodity hiring problem — the right candidates are available via standard job postings (e.g., Indeed) — and the real constraint is not finding candidates but building the filtering and onboarding process that screens for coachability and work ethic.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: for this caliber of salesperson, they're everywhere
conditions: High-volume, inbound, script-driven sales role
Real estate wholesaling or comparable sector
claims.mechanism: When the role requires adherence to a defined script rather than consultative judgment, the required trait set (coachability + work ethic) is widely available; the scarcity is in identifying those traits efficiently, not in the talent pool size.
bounded_by: Does not apply to senior sales roles or roles requiring specialized domain expertise or complex relationship sales

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the churn rate of sales reps — people quitting and being fired — in a high-volume team of 17 is approximately equal to the hiring rate, producing a steady-state headcount that masks the underlying process problem and keeps the team permanently stuck.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: the amount of churn you have of people quitting leaving is about equal to what you have to hire and that's what keeps you stuck
conditions: Midsize sales team (15-20 reps)
No culture or process enforcement
High-volume inbound model
claims.mechanism: Without process enforcement, the team continuously loses low-performers and occasional high-performers who leave for better culture; constant replacement hiring consumes management bandwidth that could otherwise be spent improving the process.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when post-peak-month revenue crashes recur and the underlying cause is identified as sales culture rather than tactics, the solution requires a willingness to fire 50% or more of the existing team to reset the culture — and that half-measures applied to an entrenched culture typically fail.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: you have to probably gut things
conditions: Leader and team have been given the tactical checklist
Script and training process have been implemented but reps continue to deviate
Cultural issue is confirmed as entrenched
claims.mechanism: Entrenched culture is self-reinforcing; reps who have operated under a mercenary model for an extended period have internalized that process non-compliance has no consequences, and partial enforcement does not change the underlying behavioral norm.
bounded_by: First step is to attempt the tactical checklist; gut-and-rebuild is only indicated if the checklist fails due to cultural resistance

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in a high-volume inbound sales business, the entire business is functionally a media-buying plus sales operation — with no meaningful delivery complexity — which means sales process excellence is the single strategic constraint and must be treated as the primary business priority.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: you buy media, you sell shit
conditions: Inbound lead generation via paid media (Google Ads)
No complex fulfillment or delivery (e.g., real estate wholesaling assignment fees)
bounded_by: Does not apply to businesses with substantial delivery complexity

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the frame set during the hiring and sales process for new reps determines whether the company attracts and retains winners: a militant, non-negotiable frame (this is exactly how we operate, get in or get out) self-selects for compliance-oriented high-effort candidates and repels mercenaries.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Hiring process for high-volume sales roles
claims.mechanism: The rigidity of the hiring process itself communicates culture before a rep ever starts; candidates who self-select out during a demanding hiring process are exactly the people who would resist script enforcement on the job.

examples.text: A real estate wholesaling business with 17 reps, Google Ads inbound leads, and a compensation structure of $42K base salary plus 10% of assignment fees was averaging $450K/month in gross revenue, had achieved $700K months in the same year, but repeatedly crashed to $200-250K in the month immediately following a peak month. The owner attributed the crashes to reps taking it easy after earning $20K-$30K in a single big month. Alex Hormozi diagnosed the root cause as a sales ops and culture problem: scripts were being treated as guidelines rather than requirements, there was no daily training cadence, and the sales manager was not enforcing process consistency. The business had consistent inbound lead quality month to month, ruling out lead variation as the cause.

Alex Hormozi described the group screening funnel for high-volume sales hires: applicants receive the script before the interview and are asked to submit a 60-second video reciting it. Those who submit are invited to a group call (not individual calls, to save management time), where they are drilled on the script and given feedback. If a candidate takes the feedback and improves their delivery, they advance to a job offer call. If a candidate shows up unprepared or reacts with attitude to coaching feedback, they are immediately eliminated from consideration. From a starting group of ten candidates, two or three typically advance to job offers. Hormozi noted that the rigor of the process itself communicates the company's culture and filters for coachability and work ethic.

practices.text: Send candidates the sales script before any interview and require a 60-second video of them reciting it as the first screening gate.
Run group coaching calls (not individual interviews) for sales rep candidates; drill the script live, give feedback, and observe whether candidates are coachable.
Require script memorization and verbatim delivery as a non-negotiable condition of employment for all reps; treat deviation as a fireable offense.
Conduct daily sales training sessions to keep all reps delivering the script correctly and consistently.
practices.topic: Daily Sales Role-Play as Training Cadence

practices.text: Before attempting a cultural gut-and-rebuild, implement the full sales ops checklist (script enforcement, daily training, daily huddles, career goal conversations); only escalate to mass termination if the checklist fails due to entrenched cultural resistance.

Hold the sales manager accountable as the primary lever for team performance — if the team has persistent culture problems, diagnose whether the manager is reinforcing the right behaviors before making rep-level changes.
practices.topic: Leadership Sets Culture; Culture Determines Acceptable Effort
